ANT-15: add repo-health-check skill for periodic workflow audit (#16)

New skill (.cursor/skills/repo-health-check/SKILL.md) provides a
structured protocol for auditing repo workflow instructions, conventions,
skills, and project metadata. Covers 7 check categories:

1. Cross-reference consistency between AGENTS.md, conventions, and skills
2. Stale task notes (PLAN.md, TODO.md, WORKLOG.md)
3. Project README accuracy (status, run instructions, deps)
4. Skill freshness (references to current conventions)
5. Linear sync (issue statuses vs actual git state)
6. Orphaned artifacts (dead files, empty directories)
7. Convention coherence (catch-all for gaps and contradictions)

Includes explicit skip criteria to avoid wasting time on cosmetic changes.

commit.message:
fix: getReportsByDates crashes when queried date has no report items (#154)

* fix: getReportsByDates crashes when queried date has no report items

When any queried date had no report items in DynamoDB, the map callback
returned undefined (implicit return when result.Items was falsy). The
subsequent forEach then accessed .date on undefined, causing a TypeError
crash.

Fix: always return { date, reports } from the map callback, using
nullish coalescing (result.Items ?? []) to default to an empty array.

Added unit tests for ReportsAdapter.getReportsByDates covering:
- dates with items, dates without items, mixed dates, empty dates array
Added handler tests for get-by-dates covering:
- valid dates, empty results, missing params, adapter errors

Resolves #153

commit.message:
[#305] Implement Gazebo robot MCP server (#325)

## Summary

Implement a robot-focused FastMCP server for the Dr.QP Gazebo
environment. This adds host-side and devcontainer-side control paths for
booting and shutting down the robot, reading robot and world state, and
recording robot state snapshots over time.

## Changes

### Features
- Add a new `drqp_robot_mcp` Python package that exposes a FastMCP
server for robot lifecycle and simulation inspection workflows.
- Add MCP tools to boot the robot to `torque_on`, shut it down through
the lifecycle state machine, read the latest robot state, record state
snapshots, and fetch Gazebo world entity poses.
- Register the new MCP server in the workspace MCP configuration so it
can be launched from the repository virtual environment.

### Configuration
- Add package metadata and console entrypoint so the MCP server can be
installed and run from the existing Python environment.
- Add the new package to the repository requirements so editable
installs include the MCP server.

### Tests
- Add unit coverage for the host-side controller lifecycle and recording
behavior.
- Add unit coverage for parsing Gazebo pose output into structured
world-state data.

### Documentation
- Add package README covering installation, startup, and the supported
MCP capabilities.

## Technical Details

### Approach
- The implementation is split into a host-side controller and a
devcontainer-side bridge.
- The host-side controller is responsible for starting the devcontainer
service, invoking bridge commands, coordinating lifecycle transitions,
and managing in-memory robot state recording.
- The bridge runs inside the devcontainer so ROS 2 and Gazebo operations
can execute in the environment where those tools are available.
- Robot state is assembled from ROS topics, while world state is
assembled from Gazebo pose output plus the ROS clock.

### Dependencies
- Add `mcp[cli]>=1.26.0` for the FastMCP server and CLI runtime.

### Assumptions
1. The existing devcontainer compose service is the correct execution
environment for ROS 2 and Gazebo operations.
2. The robot lifecycle can be driven through the existing `/robot_event`
and `/robot_state` topics.
3. Running the MCP server from the repository virtual environment is the
intended local workflow.

## Testing Strategy

### Unit Tests
- Added controller tests covering boot-up, shutdown, and state recording
flows.
- Added parser tests covering Gazebo pose message extraction.

### Integration and CI
- PR checks currently show successful docs, validation, reformat, and
Codecov runs.
- An additional `Agent` check is still in progress.

### Manual Testing
- Not documented in the branch changes.
- I have not run this PR branch locally from the current workspace
session because the local checkout is on a different branch.

## Breaking Changes

No breaking changes expected.

## Risk Assessment

### Risks
- The MCP server depends on Docker Compose, the devcontainer service,
and ROS/Gazebo tooling being available and healthy at runtime.
- Lifecycle transitions assume the current robot state machine topics
and event names remain stable.

### Mitigation
- The controller raises explicit errors when container commands fail or
when expected state transitions do not occur.
- Unit tests cover the controller flow and the Gazebo pose parsing logic
to reduce regression risk in the core orchestration code.

## Related

- Closes #305

## Checklist

- [x] Code follows project conventions
- [x] Tests added for new logic
- [x] Documentation updated
- [x] No breaking changes expected
- [ ] Manual runtime verification completed against a live Gazebo
session

  of <PortalHeaderActions /> inside .workspace-shell-header__meta

  prevent double-rendering on desktop

effectively unusable for testing since even small batches consumed a large
portion of the shared quota.

Add an explicit bulk_recall_batch: 5 specific limit for sandbox, matching the
pattern used for other premium APIs (market_value, history, lien_theft).
This gives sandbox users a small dedicated quota for testing the bulk recall
batch endpoint without depleting their shared lifetime allowance.

* Fix: add 'recalls-batch' to DashboardSidebarProps messages type

The messages type in DashboardSidebarProps did not include 'recalls-batch',
so when the bulkRecallsFrontend flag was enabled and the nav item was added,
messages['recalls-batch'] resolved to undefined at runtime due to the
'as keyof typeof messages' cast, resulting in a blank sidebar label.

* Fix overage billing to only charge for VINs that exceed quota in bulk requests

Previously, when a bulk request pushed usage past the limit, the entire
batch was billed at the overage rate. For example, a 100-VIN batch where
only 5 VINs exceeded the included quota would charge overage for all 100.

Now the overage count is correctly computed as
Math.min(count, currentUsage - limit), so only the VINs that actually
exceed the included quota are billed as overage.

* fix: update API key handling in recalls-batch landing client

- Changed variable name from 'key' to 'keyData' for clarity.
- Updated session API key assignment to use 'keyData.apiKey' instead of 'key' to ensure correct value is set.

* fix: enhance error handling in notifyWebhook for bulk recall batch

- Added detailed logging for rejected webhook URLs, including the URL and batch ID.
- Integrated Sentry exception capturing to track validation errors with additional context for better debugging.

* fix: remove fire-and-forget poller kick from submit handler

The void runRecallsBatchPollerCore() call in the submit handler ran the
full poller (iterating ALL active jobs, downloading Dropbox files, parsing
CSVs, writing Firestore batches) as fire-and-forget within the submit
Cloud Function instance. Since the response is sent immediately after,
the function could be terminated mid-write, leaving jobs stuck in
'processing' state.

The poller is already properly triggered by the AutoRecalls webhook
(autorecallsWebhook.ts) which awaits the poller after sending a 200
response, ensuring the function stays alive until completion. The
immediate kick was also unlikely to find results for the just-submitted
batch since AutoRecalls still needs to process the uploaded CSV.

* fix: use permissive regex in parseVinsCsv so invalid CSV VINs reach validation

parseVinsCsv previously used the strict VIN charset regex (no I/O/Q),
silently dropping near-miss VINs from CSV input. The same VINs submitted
via the body.vins array were not pre-filtered and would instead surface
as an invalidVins error. This inconsistency meant CSV users got silent
data loss while array users got clear error messages.

Widen the CSV extraction regex to any 17-char alphanumeric string so
that all VIN candidates — regardless of input method — reach the
downstream isValidVin check and produce consistent error behavior.

* Fix overage billing double-charge race condition for concurrent bulk requests

Use Firestore transactions in recordLookup to atomically read pre-increment
usage values before applying the increment. Pass these pre-increment values
through to createNewSubscriptionUsageRecord so each request computes overage
based on its own isolated baseline rather than the inflated global counter.

Previously, concurrent bulk requests could both read the fully-incremented
currentUsage from Firestore and each bill for the full cumulative overage,
leading to double-charging. Now each request sees only its own share of
units that crossed the limit.

* Fix: fall back to monthlyPreIncrement when lifetime transaction fails

When isPermanent is true and the lifetime usage Firestore transaction
fails, lifetimePreIncrement was left at its zero-initialized default.
This zero value was returned to downstream billing, causing incorrect
overage calculations. Now the catch block assigns monthlyPreIncrement
as the fallback so billing sees accurate prior usage.

* fix: re-add scheduled poller as safety net for webhook timeout

The AutoRecalls webhook runs runRecallsBatchPollerCore() inline after
responding 200. For large batches (10k VINs), downloading from Dropbox,
parsing CSV, and writing thousands of Firestore docs could exceed the
540s Cloud Function timeout.

Re-add recallsBatchPoller (every 5 minutes) as a fallback. The poller
core is idempotent — it only processes jobs still in 'processing' state
and tracks claimed result files. If the webhook times out mid-work, the
next scheduled tick picks up where it left off.

Architecture is now:
- AutoRecalls webhook: fast-path, tries to process immediately
- Scheduled poller: safety net, catches anything the webhook missed
- Both call the same runRecallsBatchPollerCore() function

* fix: reduce scheduled poller frequency from 5min to 30min for cost

The scheduled poller is a safety net, not the primary processing path.
The AutoRecalls webhook handles ~99% of batches immediately. The poller
only fires when a webhook invocation times out on a large batch.

At 30-min intervals: ~1,440 invocations/month (vs ~8,640 at 5min).
Most invocations exit after a single Firestore query finding zero
active jobs (~100ms, minimal cost).

* Enhance bulk recall batch processing and validation

- Introduced isBulkRecallBatchVinCandidate function to validate VINs more consistently across different input methods.
- Updated recallsBatchSubmitHandler to collect and return invalid VINs in the response.
- Refactored webhook handling in autorecallsWebhook to use Pub/Sub for asynchronous processing.
- Improved error handling and logging in notifyWebhook and other functions.
- Added metadata generation for bulk recall batch landing page with translations and keywords.
- Updated CSV parsing to utilize the new VIN validation function for better consistency.

* fix: use null (isGated) instead of 0 for Starter tier bulk_recall_batch limit

Setting bulk_recall_batch: 0 caused Starter-tier users to see a confusing
'API limit exceeded' error instead of the proper 'not available for your tier'
message. Changing to null triggers the isGated mechanism in getApiLimits,
which returns the clearer API_NOT_AVAILABLE_FOR_TIER error.

* fix: validate result file VINs against job before committing results

The poller matched Dropbox result files to batch jobs using only
time-based ordering, which could deliver incorrect recall data if the
partner processed batches out of submission order.

After parsing the CSV, verify that at least 50% of the result file's
VINs appear in the job's VIN list. If the ratio is below the threshold,
release the Dropbox result claim so the correct batch can pick it up on
the next poll cycle.

* test: add unit tests for recalls batch API handlers

Introduced comprehensive unit tests for the recalls batch API handlers, including submit, status, results, and download functionalities. The tests mock dependencies and validate various scenarios, ensuring robust error handling and correct responses. This addition enhances the overall test coverage and reliability of the recalls batch processing system.

commit.message:
feat(seer): Add dual-read helpers for Seer project preferences from Sentry DB (#111591)

relates to AIML-2611, AIML-2610

Add `read_preference_from_sentry_db()` and
`bulk_read_preferences_from_sentry_db()` helpers to read Seer project
preferences directly from Sentry DB instead of Seer API. This is the
foundation for cutting over reads behind the
`organizations:seer-project-settings-read-from-sentry` feature flag.

We don't actually use the helpers in this PR. WIP followup PR:
https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/pull/111594

### Extra

We return None if a project is unconfigured. Matching Seer's behavior
([single](https://github.com/getsentry/seer/blob/3cec03cac3abd9f1f26ac42296d6c870669c66f0/src/seer/automation/preferences.py#L54),
[bulk](https://github.com/getsentry/seer/blob/3cec03cac3abd9f1f26ac42296d6c870669c66f0/src/seer/automation/preferences.py#L98)),
"unconfigured" means we don't have any SeerProjectRepository rows _and_
none of the project options are set.

We use `project.get_option` and `ProjectOptions.objects.isset` for
single reads, and `ProjectOption.objects.get_value_bulk_id` for bulk
read. Why:
1. `project.get_option` and `ProjectOptions.objects.isset` use cache.
For a single project we want to check the cache first.
2. `ProjectOption.objects.get_value_bulk_id` is one DB query. For our
bulk use cases (`OrganizationAutofixAutomationSettingsEndpoint`
get/post, `configure_seer_for_existing_org`) where we may be querying
many projects for a single org, we do max 5 option queries, one for each
project option. It also returns None for missing options so we can
easily tell which projects have configured options and which don't.
